segments.s334-g33: بدو (prepositional pronoun) precedes the verb and means “to him/unto him.” داد is the past-tense verb “gave.” کردش is کرد + enclitic object -ش (“him/it”), here functioning as a past-tense verb meaning “made/said” (literally “did to him”); together داد و کردش = “gave to him and did/made (for him).” بسی is a quantifier placed before a noun meaning “much/very,” and آفرین is a noun/exclamation “praise/’bravo’,” so بسی آفرین = “much praise” (i.e., “praised him profusely”).
segments.s334-g34: که is the complementizer ‘that’ introducing reported speech. از تو literally ‘from you’ here functions idiomatically as ‘in you’ or ‘of you.’ مبادا is an optative/negative particle ‘may it not be’ or ‘lest’ (a wish that something not happen); the verb ‘be’ is omitted, so مبادا + nominal phrase = ‘may there not be….’ دلی is the noun ‘a heart,’ پر ز = ‘full of’ (پر + the poetic preposition ز), and کین is an archaic/poetic noun ‘vengeance, hatred.’ Altogether: ‘that—may there not be in you a heart full of hatred.’
segments.s334-g1: "چو" is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The clause uses classical/poetic word order: subject (گودرز) + verb (بنشست) 'Goudarz sat down', then a new clause with verb first (برخاست) followed by the subject (طوس) — a common order in epic Persian meaning 'Tous rose'.
segments.s334-g2: بشد here functions like 'he went / presented himself' (literal root 'became/it happened'), followed by the prepositional phrase پیش خسرو 'before Khosrow' (پیش = 'in the presence of'). زمین داد بوس is an idiomatic sequence: داد ('gave') + بوس ('kiss') — literally 'gave a kiss to the earth' = 'kissed the ground' (an act of prostration); the subject (طوس) is carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s334-g3: بدو is the prepositional/clitic form 'to him/unto him' (introducing the indirect object). گفت here is the past-tense verb 'he said' (the lexical senses given list a noun but context shows the verb usage). شاها is a vocative 'O king!'. انوشه بدی is a short wish/phrase attached as the content of the speech; بدی is used in the sense 'evil/misfortune' here (see next segment for explicit distancing).
segments.s334-g4: همیشه 'always' sets the temporal scope. ز تو 'from you' (ز = 'from'), دور دست together indicate 'far away / remote' (دور = 'distant', دست = 'hand' used idiomatically to mean 'out of reach'); بدی again is 'evil/misfortune'. The whole clause is a conventional blessing/wish: 'may misfortune always be far from you.'
segments.s334-g5: منم is the emphatic first-person 'I am' / 'I too' (here copular/predicate). The preposition ز 'from/of' introduces the genitive phrase 'of these بزرگان (great ones)'. فریدون نژاد is a compound attributive meaning 'of the lineage/stock of Fereydun' (نژاد = 'lineage').
segments.s334-g6: ز نام‌آوران 'of the name-bringers' = 'of the renowned/celebrated ones' (نام = 'fame/reputation', آوران = 'bearers/bringers'). تا introduces a temporal clause 'until', and بیامد قباد is 'Qobad came' with the verb (بیامد) before the subject (قباد), a frequent poetic ordering: 'until Qobad arrived.'
segments.s334-g7: کمر بسته‌ام is an idiomatic perfect form 'I have girded my waist/loins' (بسته‌ام = بسته + the 1sg enclitic -ام 'I have bound'), meaning 'I have prepared myself' or 'I have taken up arms'; پیش ایرانیان is the prepositional phrase 'before the Iranians / in the presence of the Iranians.'
segments.s334-g8: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'such that/so that / and ...'. نگشادم is a negated perfect/past form (ن‑ + گشادم) meaning 'I have not loosened/opened' or here 'I have not undone/relaxed' (the lexical senses list 'opening/relieving' but context shows a negative past sense). از بند 'from the bond(s)/fetter(s)' (بند = 'bond/fetter'), هرگز 'never', میان 'among/in the midst' — together: 'that I have never loosened from the bonds among them.'
segments.s334-g9: This is a prepositional/locative phrase in poetic word order: به + کوه هماون marks the place 'on/at Mount Homaun', followed by ز جوشن تنم which literally reads 'from the cuirass my-body' — i.e., 'the cuirass about my body' (تنم = تن + possessive -م). هماون is a place/name and ز is the poetic form of از 'from, of'. The verb that completes the clause (بخست) comes in the next segment.
segments.s334-g10: بخست is a past, poetic form of بستن (to fasten/close) meaning 'clasped/fastened.' The coordinating و links to the next clause; همان is a demonstrative 'that very' pointing back to the cuirass, بود is the past copula 'was', and پیراهنم is 'my shirt' (پیراهن + possessive -م). Together they state that the cuirass acted as his garment.
segments.s334-g11: به کین سیاوش functions as a purpose/motive phrase 'for the vengeance of Siyâvash' (به + کین + proper name). بر آن رزمگاه is a locative phrase 'on that battlefield' with بر = 'on/upon' and آن رزمگاه = 'that battlefield.' The whole segment sets time/place and motive; the verb clause follows.
segments.s334-g12: بدم here functions as the main verbal element meaning roughly 'I was/kept' (a first-person action; an older/poetic form). هر شبی is an adverbial phrase 'every night' (هر = 'each/every', شبی = 'a night'), and پاسبان سپاه is a nominal phrase 'the army's guard' where پاسبان = 'guard' and سپاه = 'army.' Word order places the verb before the temporal and nominal complements, typical in classical poetry.
segments.s334-g13: به لاون marks the location 'at/into Lavān' (place-name). سپه را is the definite direct object 'the army' with the object marker را. نکردم is the negative past of کردن 'I did not do' and here means 'I did not release/abandon' with رها as its complement: رها = 'free/released.' The clause means 'I did not set the army free at Lavān.'
segments.s334-g14: همی بودم is an emphatic/continuous past construction 'I was / I kept being' (همی gives continuity/emphasis, بودم = 'I was'). اندر دم اژدها is a locative phrase 'in the dragon's breath/mouth' (اندر = 'in/within', دم = 'breath/moment', اژدها = 'dragon'), meaning he remained even in the immediate presence/peril of the dragon.
segments.s334-g15: به مازندران here gives the location 'in/at Mazandaran.' بسته is a past-participial/adjectival form meaning 'bound/tied,' so the clause reads 'Kavus was bound in Mazandaran' with کاووس as the subject and بود the copula indicating past state.
segments.s334-g16: دگر بند = 'another fetter/bond' (دگر = 'other/another', بند = 'fetter/bond'). بر گردن طوس is a prepositional phrase 'upon Tous's neck' (بر = 'on/upon', گردن = 'neck', طوس = the hero Tous), and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause states that, while Kavus was bound in Mazandaran, Tous too bore another fetter.
segments.s334-g17: نکردم is the past negative first-person singular of کردن (I did not / I did not make). In classical Persian the verb can come first; سپه را is the definite/direct object (را marks it). به جایی یله is a prepositional phrase 'to/into any place loose' (یله = slack/loose) and describes how the army was not left — together: 'I did not leave the army loose anywhere.'
segments.s334-g18: نه ... هرگز is a negation framing the clause; نه begins the negation and هرگز reinforces it ('never'). از من ('from me' = concerning me/against me) marks the party affected. کسی here is negated by نه ... هرگز and functions as 'no one.' کرد گله is a light verb construction meaning 'made complaint' (کرد = made/did, گله = complaint). Altogether: 'nor did anyone ever make a complaint against me.'
segments.s334-g19: کنون is an adverb 'now.' شاه is the subject 'the king.' سیر here is adjectival/predicative 'sated' (i.e. satisfied, full) and modifies the subject; آمد is the past-tense verb 'came.' از تاج و گنج is a source/measure phrase 'from (his) crown and treasure' — lit. 'sated from crown and treasure.'
segments.s334-g20: همی is a classical progressive/emphatic particle (marks continuing or imminent action). بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form of گذشتن used as a simple present/future third-person singular 'passes / will pass.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' این is 'this.' سرای in the sense-list given does not match the usual meaning 'house/abode' (see senses below), so its specific lexical sense here is not listed; سپنج follows as the name/descriptor of the place. The phrase means 'he will pass from/by this سرای سپنج.'
segments.s334-g21: چه is an interrogative/exclamative 'what.' فرمایدم is an archaic/polite verb form meaning roughly 'shall I say / I say' (here rhetorical 'what can I say'); the form is classical rather than modern. چیست is the fused interrogative/copula 'what is' (چی + ست, with ست = archaic copula 'is'). نیروی من is a genitive phrase 'my strength/my power.' Altogether: 'What shall I say? What is my power?'
segments.s334-g22: تو دانی is present second-person singular 'you know.' هنرها و آهوی من is a coordinated object: 'my arts/skills and my āhū' (the possessive pronoun من after the second noun covers both nouns as 'my ...'). In poetic usage āhū can be literal 'gazelle' or figurative 'beloved'; context decides which sense is intended.
segments.s334-g23: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' داد is the past-tense verb 'gave.' پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply.' بدو is the indirect object 'to him.' شهریار is the subject 'the king' (classical word order allows the subject to follow these elements). The whole line reads 'Thus the king gave him an answer.'
segments.s334-g24: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). بیش here means 'greater/excessive' and is predicative; است is the copula 'is.' رنج تو is 'your suffering/hardship' (رنج = suffering, تو = your). از روزگار means 'from/of fate/time' (here 'from fate' or 'at the hands of fate'). Note classical inversion: the predicate 'بیش است' appears before the subject phrase 'رنج تو از روزگار,' producing 'that your suffering from fate is excessive.'
segments.s334-g25: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'ever' or 'always' placed before the verb. باش is the imperative/jussive 'be!' addressed to the addressee. با + کاویانی درفش is an accompaniment phrase 'with the Kayanian banner' (کاویانی = Kayanian/Kavian, درفش = banner); together the segment reads 'always be with the Kayanian banner.'
segments.s334-g26: تو is the explicit subject pronoun 'you' used for emphasis. باشی is an archaic subjunctive/jussive form roughly functioning as 'may you be' or an imperative. سپهدار is a predicate noun 'commander' (title); زرینه modifies the following noun phrase as 'golden' and applies to کفش here, so سپهدار زرینه کفش describes a commander 'of/with golden shoes/boots.' Word order places the emphasized subject before the verb and then the title and descriptive phrase.
segments.s334-g27: بدین is a fused locative/adverbial ('به' + 'این') meaning 'in/at/by this.' مرز گیتی is a noun–noun construction 'border/edge of the world' (مرز modified by گیتی). خراسان is the subject and تراست is the predicative construction meaning 'is yours' (تراست = 'to you is' / 'belongs to you'); overall: 'By this border of the world, Khorasan is yours.'
segments.s334-g28: از این نامداران is a prepositional phrase 'of/from these renowned ones' or 'among these famous men.' تن‌آسان is a nominal phrase 'easy-bodied' or idiomatically 'one with an easy/comfortable life.' تراست again means 'is yours' — the clause states that, among these famed men, the comfortable life (or the fortunate place) belongs to you.
segments.s334-g29: نبشتند is the archaic past tense plural 'they wrote/they inscribed' (an older form of نوشتند). عهدی is the direct object 'a covenant/pledge.' بر آن هم نشان is a locative phrase 'upon that same sign/token' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, هم = emphatic 'same/also', نشان = sign/token).
segments.s334-g30: به پیش is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of' or 'before.' بزرگان is 'the great ones' (elders/nobles) and گردنکشان literally 'neck‑turners' is an epithet meaning 'proud/insolent ones' or 'rebels' depending on context; together the phrase places the action 'before the proud great ones.'
segments.s334-g31: نهادند is past plural 'they placed/they set' (verb). The doubled بر ('بر عهد بر مهر زر') stacks locative/circumstantial phrases: نهادند بر عهد = 'they placed on the covenant' and بر مهر زر = 'on/over a gold seal' (مهر = seal, زر = gold). The sequence describes where and how they affixed the pledge.
segments.s334-g32: یکی is the numeral 'one' used here as 'a single' or 'one (item).' طوق زرین 'a golden torque/collar' names the first item; و introduces the second item, زرین کمر 'a golden girdle/belt.' زرین is the adjective 'golden' applying to both طوق and کمر; parallel structure lists the two precious gifts placed on the seal.

sentences.s334-s17: He gave it to him and praised him profusely, saying, "May you never have a heart full of hatred."
sentences.s334-s1: When Goudarz sat down, Tous rose and went before Khosrow and kissed the ground.
sentences.s334-s2: He said to him, 'O king, may evil (misfortune) be kept far from you always.'
sentences.s334-s3: I too am of these great ones, of the stock of Fereydun, of the renowned until Qobad arrived.
sentences.s334-s4: I have girded my loins before the Iranians and have never loosened (undone) my bonds in their midst.
sentences.s334-s5: On Mount Homaun the cuirass clasped my body, and that very cuirass served as my shirt.
sentences.s334-s6: For the vengeance of Siyâvash, on that battlefield I kept watch over the army each night.
sentences.s334-s7: At Lavān I did not let the army go free; I remained even in the dragon's breath.
sentences.s334-s8: Kavus was bound in Mazandaran; another fetter was upon Tous's neck.
sentences.s334-s9: I did not leave the army loose in any place; no one ever complained about me.
sentences.s334-s10: Now the king, sated with crown and treasure, has come and will pass by this sponge-house.
sentences.s334-s11: What can I say? What is my strength? You know my arts and my beloved.
sentences.s334-s12: Thus the king answered him: that your hardship at the hands of fate is too great.
sentences.s334-s13: Always remain with the Kayanian banner; may you be a commander wearing golden boots.
sentences.s334-s14: In this part of the world, Khorasan is yours; of these renowned men an easy life is yours.
sentences.s334-s15: They inscribed a covenant upon that very token before the proud great ones.
sentences.s334-s16: They placed upon the covenant, upon a golden seal, one golden torque and a golden girdle.
